metadata.project_title: Synthetic Tabular Data Generation at the Intersection of Privacy, Fidelity, and Rare Events: A Diffusion-Based Approach

metadata.title: Classifier-Free Diffusion Guidance
metadata.authors: Jonathan Ho, Tim Salimans
metadata.venue: NeurIPS 2021 Workshop on Deep Generative Models and Downstream Applications

papers.abstract: This paper introduces classifier-free guidance, a method that improves diffusion model sample fidelity without requiring an external classifier. By jointly training a conditional and unconditional diffusion model and linearly combining their score estimates, the authors show that diffusion models can achieve the same fidelity-diversity trade-off as classifier-guided models, but with a simpler and more efficient training pipeline. The method establishes that pure generative diffusion models can produce state-of-the-art image samples without adversarial classifier gradients.
papers.problem_statement: While classifier guidance effectively boosts sample fidelity in diffusion models, it complicates training by requiring a separate classifier trained on noisy intermediate representations. Moreover, classifier-guided diffusion can be seen as adversarially exploiting classifier gradients rather than improving genuine sample quality. The paper seeks a way to perform guidance intrinsically within the diffusion model, eliminating the need for a separate classifier while preserving or improving fidelity-diversity trade-offs.
papers.proposed_method: The proposed method, classifier-free guidance, jointly trains a conditional diffusion model pθ(x|c) and an unconditional model pθ(x) using a single network. During training, the conditioning variable is randomly dropped with probability puncond to enable both modes of learning. During sampling, conditional and unconditional score estimates are linearly combined using ε̃θ = (1 + w)εθ(z, c) - wεθ(z), where w controls guidance strength. This modification preserves the benefits of classifier guidance without requiring external classifiers, enabling adjustable trade-offs between sample fidelity and diversity.
key_contributions: Introduced classifier-free guidance, removing the need for an auxiliary classifier during diffusion guidance.
Showed that the method achieves equivalent Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) trade-offs as classifier guidance.
Demonstrated that diffusion models alone can control fidelity and diversity via internal score mixing.
Simplified the generative pipeline by unifying conditional and unconditional diffusion training in a single model.
Provided theoretical and empirical analysis showing that classifier-free guidance does not rely on adversarial gradients.
evaluation_metrics: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
Inception Score (IS)

main_results.image_quality: Classifier-free guidance achieved FID = 2.43 and IS = 158.47 on ImageNet 128x128, outperforming classifier-guided ADM-G and BigGAN-deep baselines.
main_results.guidance_tradeoff: FID decreases monotonically with weak guidance (w ≈ 0.3), while IS increases with stronger guidance (w ≥ 4), replicating the classic fidelity-diversity trade-off.
main_results.efficiency: Joint training reduced model complexity, though sampling requires two forward passes per step (for conditional and unconditional scores).
main_results.simplicity: The entire method requires only a single line of code modification compared to standard diffusion training.

limitations: Sampling speed is slower due to dual forward passes for conditional and unconditional scores.
Does not include formal privacy or robustness guarantees.
Trade-off control (parameter w) is empirical and dataset-specific.
Evaluation limited to image generation tasks; generalization to other modalities untested.
papers.relevance_justification: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Classifier-free guidance provides a key conceptual foundation for controllable diffusion sampling, enabling fidelity-diversity balancing without auxiliary networks. This concept parallels privacy-utility trade-offs in tabular diffusion, where conditional and unconditional score mixing could regulate privacy exposure versus data fidelity, making it methodologically significant for privacy-preserving tabular synthesis.

metadata.title: CoDi Co-evolving contrastive diffusion models for mixed-type tabular synthesis
CoDi- Co-evolving Contrastive Diffusion Models for Mixed-type Tabular Synthesis
Conditional Score-Based Diffusion Models for Bayesian Inference in Infinite Dimensions
metadata.authors: Lorenzo Baldassari, Ali Siahkoohi, Josselin Garnier, Knut Sølna, and Maarten V. de Hoop

papers.abstract: This paper introduces a theoretically grounded framework for performing Bayesian inference in infinite-dimensional function spaces using conditional score-based diffusion models (SDMs). Unlike previous approaches that rely on unconditional scores or finite-dimensional projections, the authors define and analyze the conditional score in infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. They provide convergence guarantees for sampling from the posterior distribution of Bayesian linear inverse problems and demonstrate that their approach is discretization-invariant. Theoretical derivations are supported by stylized and large-scale numerical experiments, including seismic imaging tasks.
papers.problem_statement: Score-based diffusion models have shown great success in finite-dimensional inverse problems but lack a rigorous framework for Bayesian inference in infinite-dimensional function spaces. Existing methods are often heuristic, computationally expensive, and fail to preserve discretization invariance. This work aims to define and prove the validity of conditional score-based diffusion models in infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces for accurate, scalable, and theoretically consistent Bayesian inference.
papers.proposed_method: The authors introduce a conditional score function S(t, x, y) defined directly in infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, enabling posterior sampling without projection or discretization. The forward-reverse stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework is extended to conditional settings, supported by rigorous proofs for well-posedness and convergence. The method employs amortized conditional SDMs, where a neural network learns the conditional score using a conditional denoising score matching objective. The implementation leverages Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) for function-space learning, ensuring discretization invariance and scalability to large problems.
key_contributions: Defined the conditional score function for infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and proved its convergence properties.
Extended the forward-reverse SDE framework for conditional diffusion in function spaces.
Derived theoretical guarantees for well-posedness under Gaussian and general prior measures.
Proved that the conditional denoising score matching objective is a consistent estimator of the conditional score.
Demonstrated discretization-invariant Bayesian inference through stylized and large-scale numerical experiments.
datasets_used: Stylized synthetic dataset for demonstrating conditional distributions in function space.
Large-scale seismic imaging dataset (Parihaka 3D marine seismic survey) for validating posterior inference and uncertainty quantification.
evaluation_metrics: Posterior mean (minimum variance estimator)
Pointwise standard deviation for uncertainty quantification
Qualitative comparison of predicted conditional distributions and discretization-invariance behavior

main_results.theoretical_findings: The paper proves that the reverse SDE with the proposed conditional score converges exponentially fast to the correct posterior distribution under Gaussian priors. It also establishes sufficient conditions for uniform boundedness of the score over time.
main_results.practical_results: Numerical results show that the proposed method can capture multimodal conditional distributions and perform accurate large-scale Bayesian inference with discretization invariance. In seismic imaging, the conditional SDM achieves reliable uncertainty quantification with minimal reconstruction error.
main_results.comparative_observations: Compared to heuristic projection-based methods, the proposed approach is theoretically sound, computationally more efficient, and independent of the discretization grid.

limitations: Assumes access to sufficient paired data (X, Y) for conditional training.
Primarily validated on linear inverse problems; nonlinear extensions remain open.
Requires careful control of numerical stability due to potential singularities in the conditional score at small diffusion times.
Experimental validation limited to Hilbert-space settings; generalization to arbitrary function spaces remains theoretical.
papers.relevance_justification: Highly relevant - this paper provides deep theoretical underpinnings for conditional score-based diffusion models, expanding their applicability to infinite-dimensional Bayesian inference. While not directly focused on tabular data, its rigorous formulation of conditional diffusion and amortized inference provides valuable conceptual grounding for privacy-preserving, high-dimensional diffusion modeling frameworks applicable to structured data generation and inverse problem-solving.

metadata.title: Controllable Tabular Data Synthesis Using Diffusion Models
metadata.authors: Tongyu Liu, Ju Fan, Nan Tang, Guoliang Li, Xiaoyong Du
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data (SIGMOD), Vol. 2, No. 1, Article 28

papers.abstract: This paper introduces a diffusion-based framework for controllable tabular data synthesis, addressing the challenge of generating synthetic tables under user-specified conditions while preserving realism. Unlike traditional conditional generative models that require retraining for each new condition, the proposed approach first learns an unconditional diffusion model and then employs lightweight controllers to guide generation. The method supports both intra-table and inter-table conditional synthesis efficiently while maintaining high data fidelity.
papers.problem_statement: Existing tabular data synthesis models lack flexibility and efficiency in generating synthetic data under diverse or unseen conditions. Each condition typically requires designing and training a new conditional model, making them computationally expensive and inflexible for dynamic real-world use cases. The challenge is to develop a controllable, condition-aware synthesis method that maintains both realism and adaptability without retraining for every new condition.
papers.proposed_method: The proposed method, termed RelDDPM, separates synthesis into two stages: training an unconditional diffusion model (Diffuser) and designing condition-specific controllers. The Diffuser learns the unconditional joint distribution of real tabular data using analog bit encoding to handle mixed (categorical + numerical) features. The controllers, trained via contrastive learning, guide the Diffuser during the reverse diffusion process using correlation-aware gradients. This allows controllable generation based on intra-table (attribute-based) or inter-table (relational) conditions without re-training the base model.
key_contributions: Introduced RelDDPM, a novel controllable diffusion framework for tabular data synthesis supporting arbitrary new conditions.
Designed an unconditional DDPM for mixed-type tabular data using analog bit encoding for categorical features.
Developed correlation-aware controllers based on contrastive learning for both intra-table and inter-table conditioning.
Proposed a flexible and efficient approach that allows control injection at generation time, not training time.
Extensively evaluated on multiple real-world datasets, showing superior performance and controllability.
datasets_used: WeatherAUS - 10 years of weather data, 78% majority class
Heart - 200,000 tuples, 14 attributes across Patient and Cardio tables
Airbnb - 104,072 tuples, 26 attributes across Landlord, Neighborhood, Apartment tables
IMDB - multi-table dataset with Movie, Director, Actor relations
evaluation_metrics: Fidelity: Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) and Wasserstein Distance between real and synthetic distributions
Utility: F1-score, Accuracy, and AUC for downstream classification models
Privacy: Distance to Closest Record (DCR) and membership inference robustness
Controllability: Correlation satisfaction rate between generated data and conditioning attributes
Efficiency: Training and sampling runtime comparisons

main_results.fidelity: RelDDPM achieved the lowest JSD and Wasserstein distances across all datasets, outperforming TabDDPM, CTGAN, and OCTGAN in realism.
main_results.utility: Downstream models trained on RelDDPM-generated data reached within 3% of real-data AUC on average and surpassed GAN-based models by 5-12%.
main_results.controllability: RelDDPM successfully supported both intra- and inter-table controls, maintaining over 90% condition satisfaction accuracy.
main_results.efficiency: One unconditional model supported multiple conditions without retraining, reducing total training cost by >70% compared to conditional GAN baselines.
main_results.privacy: Generated data showed higher novelty and DCR than GAN-based methods, implying improved privacy without explicit DP mechanisms.

limitations: Analog bit encoding increases data dimensionality, leading to higher memory and computation costs.
No formal differential privacy guarantees; relies on empirical privacy assessment.
Limited exploration of rare-event or extreme-condition preservation.
Controller training can be sensitive to embedding dimension and contrastive loss margin.
Currently focused on relational/tabular settings; not evaluated on temporal or streaming data.
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - RelDDPM directly advances diffusion-based synthetic tabular data generation by adding controllability, a core aspect of my research. It aligns perfectly with my focus on balancing privacy, fidelity, and rare-event fidelity, particularly as its controller-based conditioning could be extended to preserve rare-event distributions. The analog bit encoding strategy and correlation-aware guidance are key design inspirations for my work.

metadata.title: Deep unsupervised learning using nonequilibrium thermodynamics.
Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
metadata.authors: Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, and Pieter Abbeel

papers.abstract: This paper introduces Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs), a new class of generative models that define a Markov chain of latent variables to progressively transform noise into data samples. The forward diffusion process gradually adds Gaussian noise to the data, while the reverse process-parameterized by a neural network-is trained to iteratively remove noise. DDPMs achieve high-quality image synthesis, rivaling GANs and autoregressive models, and establish an equivalence between diffusion modeling and denoising score matching with Langevin dynamics.
papers.problem_statement: Before this work, diffusion probabilistic models were known theoretically but had not demonstrated competitive sample quality or efficiency. GANs and autoregressive models dominated generative modeling but suffered from instability and mode collapse. This paper addresses the gap by showing how properly parameterized diffusion models, trained via a simplified objective equivalent to denoising score matching, can achieve state-of-the-art image generation with stable training and strong theoretical guarantees.
papers.proposed_method: DDPM defines a fixed forward diffusion process that incrementally adds Gaussian noise to data samples according to a variance schedule β₁,...,β_T. The model learns a reverse-time Gaussian denoising process pθ(xt-1|xt), parameterized by a U-Net architecture, which predicts either the original signal x₀ or the noise ε. The training objective simplifies to a mean-squared error loss between true and predicted noise, making it equivalent to denoising score matching across multiple noise levels. Sampling proceeds by reversing the diffusion process step-by-step, transforming Gaussian noise into realistic data samples.
key_contributions: Established diffusion probabilistic models as a powerful class of generative models competitive with GANs and VAEs.
Proved the theoretical link between diffusion modeling and denoising score matching using Langevin dynamics.
Proposed a simplified training objective (L_simple) that improves sample quality and training stability.
Introduced progressive lossy compression interpretation and autoregressive decoding analogy.
Achieved state-of-the-art FID and Inception Scores on CIFAR-10 and LSUN datasets.
evaluation_metrics: Inception Score (IS)
Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
Negative Log-Likelihood (bits/dim)
Rate-Distortion analysis for compression perspective

main_results.fidelity: Achieved FID=3.17 and IS=9.46 on unconditional CIFAR-10, surpassing GAN baselines (e.g., SNGAN, StyleGAN2).
main_results.efficiency: Simplified training objective improved convergence and stability while requiring only 1000 diffusion steps.
main_results.likelihood: Reported negative log-likelihood ≤3.75 bits/dim, comparable to top flow-based models.
main_results.compression: Demonstrated that over half of lossless codelength encodes imperceptible details, supporting diffusion models' efficiency in lossy representation.
main_results.architecture: U-Net with self-attention and sinusoidal time embeddings produced diverse, high-quality samples comparable to ProgressiveGAN and StyleGAN2.

limitations: Sampling is computationally expensive due to the need for hundreds of denoising steps.
No explicit handling of structured or mixed-type data (focus is on continuous image domains).
Limited exploration of formal privacy guarantees or robustness.
High training cost on large datasets (1000 diffusion steps, 10+ hours training on TPUs).
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - This foundational work defines the modern diffusion model paradigm, establishing the mathematical and algorithmic backbone for subsequent diffusion-based tabular synthesis research. The equivalence between denoising score matching and diffusion inference directly influences current diffusion architectures like TabDDPM and FinDiff. Despite being image-focused, the DDPM framework provides the core generative principles-stochastic noise scheduling, reverse SDE modeling, and score-based training-that underpin my research direction in privacy-preserving, high-fidelity synthetic tabular data generation.

metadata.title: Differentially Private Federated Learning of Diffusion Models for Synthetic Tabular Data Generation
metadata.authors: Timur Sattarov, Marco Schreyer, Damian Borth

papers.abstract: This paper introduces the DP-Fed-FinDiff framework, a novel integration of Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, and Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models designed to generate high-fidelity synthetic tabular data. The framework ensures compliance with stringent privacy regulations while maintaining data utility by combining federated training across distributed clients with differentially private gradient updates. Empirical evaluations demonstrate effectiveness on multiple financial datasets with significant improvements in privacy guarantees without compromising data quality.
papers.problem_statement: The rapidly evolving landscape of financial regulations has amplified the significance of data analytics. However, detailed and sensitive financial data introduces significant privacy challenges. Real-world tabular data contains sensitive information requiring compliance with CCPA and GDPR. Despite safeguards, there remain concerns about deploying AI models due to risks of data leakage and model attacks that could expose personally identifiable information. The challenge is to generate high-quality synthetic data while ensuring strong privacy guarantees in federated settings where data cannot be centralized.
papers.proposed_method: DP-Fed-FinDiff combines three key components: (1) FinDiff diffusion model as the base architecture using multinomial diffusion for categorical features and Gaussian diffusion for numerical features; (2) Federated Learning where multiple clients train local models on their private data and a central server aggregates model parameters via Federated Averaging without accessing raw data; (3) Differential Privacy achieved by clipping gradients and adding calibrated Gaussian noise using the sampled Gaussian mechanism (SGM). The framework employs adaptive gradient clipping threshold based on gradient norm and dynamic learning rate decay (linear, exponential, logarithmic strategies tested). Each client performs Γ local optimization updates before synchronization.
key_contributions: Introduced DP-Fed-FinDiff framework combining Differential Privacy, Federated Learning and DDPMs for synthetic tabular data generation
Framework allows precise quantification and adjustment of privacy budget suited to finance industry confidentiality requirements
Comprehensive empirical evaluation on real-world financial datasets demonstrating effectiveness in privacy-compliant data synthesis
Revealed optimal trade-offs between privacy budgets, client configurations, and federated optimization strategies
Demonstrated 100 local updates as optimal balance between privacy, utility and fidelity while reducing training time from 28 to 3.5 hours
datasets_used: Credit Default dataset (30,000 customer records, 9 categorical + 13 numerical attributes)
Census Income Data (32,561 records from 1994 U.S. Census, 10 categorical + 3 numerical)
Philadelphia City Payments Data (238,894 payment records from 58 departments, 10 categorical + 1 numerical)
Marketing Data (45,211 customer records from 2008-2010, 10 categorical + 6 numerical)
evaluation_metrics: Privacy metrics: Singling Out Risk (SOR), Linkability Risk (LR), Inference Risk (IR) - aggregated as comprehensive privacy score
Utility: ML classifier performance (Random Forest, Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, AdaBoost, MLP) on synthetic training data evaluated on real test data
Fidelity: Column fidelity (Wasserstein similarity for numeric, Jensen-Shannon divergence for categorical), Row fidelity (Pearson Correlation, Theil U coefficient)
Overall fidelity computed as mean of column and row fidelity

main_results.optimal_configuration: 100 local client updates provided optimal balance between privacy, utility and fidelity in both IID and non-IID settings, reducing training time from 28h to 3.5h compared to 1000 local updates
main_results.privacy_utility_tradeoff: With moderate privacy budget (ε=1), privacy increased by 34% while utility decreased by 15% and fidelity by 14% compared to non-DP scenario (ε=∞)
main_results.privacy_risks: Lower DP budgets (ε=0.2, ε=1.0) effectively mitigated Singling Out, Linkability, and Inference risks across all datasets
main_results.client_configuration: Increasing number of federated clients from 3 to 10 enhanced privacy protection and improved utility/fidelity scores, though gains became marginal beyond 5 clients
main_results.optimization_strategies: No single federated strategy (FedAvg, FedAdam, FedProx, FedYogi) dominated across all datasets, indicating dataset-specific characteristics influence strategy effectiveness

limitations: Training requires extensive hyperparameter tuning across multiple dimensions (local updates, learning rate, privacy budget)
Balance between privacy and utility remains challenging - stronger privacy guarantees lead to utility degradation
Evaluation limited to four financial datasets; generalization to other sensitive domains not validated
Computational cost remains high despite optimizations (3.5-28 hours depending on configuration)
No explicit handling of rare events or class imbalance in datasets
Requires trust in central aggregator for federated averaging
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - This paper directly addresses the core intersection of my research: privacy-preserving diffusion models for tabular data. The integration of differential privacy with federated learning adds a crucial dimension for distributed private data scenarios common in finance and healthcare. The comprehensive evaluation of privacy-utility-fidelity tradeoffs and the detailed analysis of optimal configurations provide essential insights for my proposed framework. While rare-event preservation is not explicitly addressed, the federated approach and strong privacy guarantees align perfectly with the privacy pillar of my research. The paper provides concrete architectural choices (adaptive clipping, learning rate strategies) that can be adapted for my work.

metadata.title: Difformer Scalable (graph) transformers induced by energy constrained diffusion
Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
metadata.authors: Prafulla Dhariwal, Alex Nichol
metadata.venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)

papers.abstract: This paper demonstrates that diffusion probabilistic models can surpass state-of-the-art GANs in image generation quality. The authors introduce architectural improvements and a classifier guidance mechanism that allows diffusion models to trade off diversity for fidelity. Their approach achieves record-breaking FID scores on ImageNet datasets while maintaining strong distribution coverage and training stability.
papers.problem_statement: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have long been dominant in image synthesis but suffer from instability and mode collapse. Diffusion models, while more stable and theoretically grounded, previously underperformed in terms of sample fidelity. The authors aim to close this quality gap by improving diffusion model architectures and introducing mechanisms for fidelity-diversity control, achieving results competitive with or superior to GANs.
papers.proposed_method: The proposed method introduces an improved U-Net architecture for diffusion models, incorporating BigGAN residual blocks, multi-resolution attention, and adaptive group normalization for timestep conditioning. Additionally, the paper introduces classifier guidance, a technique that steers the denoising process using gradients from a pre-trained classifier, enabling explicit trade-offs between fidelity and diversity. The combination of architectural optimization and classifier guidance allows diffusion models to achieve state-of-the-art FIDs with as few as 25 sampling steps.
key_contributions: Introduced architectural enhancements-BigGAN-style residual blocks, adaptive group normalization, and multi-resolution attention-that significantly improve diffusion sample quality.
Proposed 'classifier guidance', a novel method for controlling the fidelity-diversity trade-off using classifier gradients.
Achieved new state-of-the-art results on ImageNet (FID 2.97 at 128x128, 4.59 at 256x256, and 7.72 at 512x512).
Demonstrated that diffusion models surpass GANs in both unconditional and conditional image synthesis tasks.
Provided comprehensive comparisons showing improved recall (diversity) and similar or better precision (fidelity) relative to GANs.
evaluation_metrics: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
Spatial FID (sFID)
Inception Score (IS)
Precision (fidelity measure)
Recall (diversity measure)

main_results.image_synthesis: Achieved FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128x128, 4.59 on 256x256, and 7.72 on 512x512, outperforming BigGAN-deep (FID 6.95 on 256x256).
main_results.diversity_vs_fidelity: Classifier guidance improved precision (0.88) while maintaining competitive recall (0.32-0.63). Gradient scaling allowed smooth trade-offs between diversity and fidelity.
main_results.efficiency: Reduced required diffusion steps to as few as 25 without major quality loss using DDIM sampling.
main_results.architecture_ablation: BigGAN-style residual blocks, multi-resolution attention, and adaptive group normalization reduced FID by up to 40%.

limitations: Sampling remains slower than GANs due to multi-step denoising (though partially mitigated by DDIM).
Classifier guidance requires labeled data; no method proposed for unlabeled or multimodal conditioning.
Model performance highly dependent on compute and hyperparameter tuning.
No formal privacy analysis-applicability to sensitive data is untested.
papers.relevance_justification: HIGHLY RELEVANT - Although focused on image synthesis, this paper established diffusion models as superior generative frameworks over GANs, influencing their adaptation for tabular and privacy-sensitive data. The concepts of classifier guidance and fidelity-diversity trade-offs directly parallel the privacy-utility-fidelity balance central to synthetic tabular data generation.

metadata.title: Diffusion models for missing value imputation in tabular data
Diffusion Models for Tabular Data Imputation and Synthetic Data Generation
Diffusion Models for Tabular Data Imputation and Synthetic Data Generation (1)
Diffusion Models for Tabular Data- Challenges, Current Progress, and Future Directions
Domain-Constrained Diffusion Models to Synthesize Tabular Data_ A Case Study in Power Systems
Federated Diffusion Modeling with Differential Privacy for Tabular Data Synthesis
metadata.authors: Timur Sattarov, Marco Schreyer, Damian Borth

papers.abstract: This paper introduces DP-FedTabDiff, a federated learning framework that integrates Differential Privacy (DP) and Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) for high-fidelity synthetic tabular data generation. Designed for decentralized environments such as finance and healthcare, the framework enables multiple clients to collaboratively train diffusion models without sharing raw data, ensuring compliance with regulations like GDPR. DP-FedTabDiff achieves strong trade-offs between privacy guarantees, data utility, and fidelity across mixed-type tabular datasets.
papers.problem_statement: In sensitive domains like finance and healthcare, data centralization is restricted by legal and ethical constraints such as GDPR and CCPA. While federated learning allows collaborative model training without raw data exchange, it remains vulnerable to model inversion and membership inference attacks. Existing tabular diffusion models like FinDiff and FedTabDiff lack formal privacy guarantees. The paper addresses the need for a generative model that jointly ensures data privacy, fidelity, and utility in federated, non-IID settings.
papers.proposed_method: DP-FedTabDiff combines differential privacy, federated learning, and diffusion-based generative modeling to synthesize tabular data with formal privacy guarantees. Each client trains a local DP-enhanced FinDiff model, where Gaussian noise is added to clipped gradients under (ε, δ)-DP constraints. Local models are aggregated using Federated Averaging (FedAvg) or its variants (FedAdam, FedProx, FedYogi) to produce a global model. The framework evaluates privacy, utility, and fidelity using both IID and non-IID data partitions, balancing privacy budgets and federated configurations.
key_contributions: Proposes the first integration of Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, and Diffusion Models for tabular data generation.
Introduces the DP-FedTabDiff architecture, enabling decentralized yet privacy-compliant generative modeling.
Establishes a quantitative framework to evaluate privacy, fidelity, and utility trade-offs using GDPR-aligned privacy risk measures.
Demonstrates effective privacy-utility-fidelity balance across four real-world datasets under varying federated and DP configurations.
Provides empirical guidelines for optimal local update frequencies and privacy budgets for federated diffusion training.
datasets_used: Credit Default Dataset (UCI, 30,000 records, 9 categorical, 13 numerical)
Census Income Dataset (UCI, 32,561 records, 10 categorical, 3 numerical)
Philadelphia City Payments Dataset (238,894 records, 10 categorical, 1 numerical)
Bank Marketing Dataset (UCI, 45,211 records, 10 categorical, 6 numerical)
evaluation_metrics: Privacy metrics: Singling Out Risk, Linkability Risk, Inference Risk (Anonymeter)
Utility metrics: Train Synthetic, Test Real (TSTR) accuracy using five classifiers (RF, DT, LR, AdaBoost, MLP)
Fidelity metrics: Wasserstein Similarity (numerical), Jensen-Shannon Divergence (categorical), Pearson Correlation (numeric pairs), Theil U coefficient (categorical pairs)
Aggregate privacy-utility-fidelity trade-off scores

main_results.privacy: DP-FedTabDiff achieves strong privacy protection at ε = 1, reducing re-identification and linkability risks by 34% compared to non-DP setups. Lower ε (0.2) further improves privacy but degrades fidelity and utility by ~20%.
main_results.utility: Synthetic-data-trained classifiers achieve 0.68-0.81 accuracy, within 10-15% of real-data baselines under moderate privacy budgets (ε = 1).
main_results.fidelity: Column- and row-level fidelity exceeds 0.8 on average for ε = 1, closely mirroring real distributions. Visual t-SNE plots confirm structural similarity between real and synthetic data.
main_results.federation: Optimal trade-off achieved at 5 clients, Γ = 100 local updates, and FedAvg optimization. More local updates (Γ > 500) degrade privacy and convergence.
main_results.scalability: Training time reduced from 28 hours (Γ = 1000) to 3.5 hours (Γ = 100) without major quality loss.

limitations: Current approach provides empirical, not theoretical, guarantees for resistance to inference attacks beyond DP.
Evaluation limited to small- and medium-scale datasets; scalability to enterprise-level multi-silo networks not yet validated.
Rare-event fidelity not explicitly analyzed despite relevance in fraud or anomaly detection tasks.
Noise addition through DP slightly reduces correlation structure realism at low ε values.
Limited exploration of adaptive DP budget allocation across heterogeneous clients.
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - This paper aligns perfectly with the core research problem. It unifies privacy (via DP), fidelity (through diffusion), and decentralized data generation (federated learning), offering empirical evaluation of privacy-utility-fidelity trade-offs. It provides methodological inspiration for multi-institutional, privacy-safe data synthesis frameworks and benchmarks critical for diffusion-based synthetic tabular data generation in regulated domains.

metadata.title: FedTabDiff- Federated Learning of Diffusion Probabilistic Models for Synthetic Mixed-Type Tabular Data Generation
FinDiff_ Diffusion Models for Financial Tabular Data Generation
Mixed-Type Tabular Data Synthesis with Score-Based Diffusion in Latent Space
metadata.authors: Hengrui Zhang, Jiani Zhang, Balasubramaniam Srinivasan, Zhengyuan Shen, Xiao Qin, Christos Faloutsos, Huzefa Rangwala, George Karypis

papers.abstract: This paper introduces TABSYN, a score-based diffusion framework for mixed-type tabular data generation. It integrates a diffusion model with a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) operating in a continuous latent space to overcome the challenges of modeling heterogeneous tabular features. TABSYN unifies numerical and categorical attributes into a shared embedding space, enabling the application of continuous diffusion processes. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets and metrics, demonstrating high-quality, diverse, and privacy-preserving synthetic data generation.
papers.problem_statement: Traditional tabular diffusion models such as TabDDPM and STaSy struggle with mixed-type data, as they either treat categorical features as continuous or require separate models for each feature type. This limits their ability to capture inter-column relationships and correlations. Moreover, existing models often require many sampling steps, resulting in high computational cost. The challenge addressed by TABSYN is to build a unified generative framework that effectively models mixed-type tabular data in a single continuous space, while improving fidelity, efficiency, and correlation accuracy.
papers.proposed_method: TABSYN employs a two-stage generative process: (1) a transformer-based Variational Autoencoder (VAE) that maps mixed-type tabular data into a structured latent space; and (2) a score-based diffusion model trained in that latent space. The VAE uses column-wise tokenizers, adaptive β-scheduling for balanced reconstruction and regularization, and transformer encoders to model inter-column relationships. In the latent space, diffusion follows a simplified variance-exploding (VE) process with linear noise σ(t)=t, reducing reverse-process approximation error. Sampling is accelerated, requiring fewer than 20 steps. The generated latent vectors are decoded back to tabular data via a detokenizer symmetric to the tokenizer.
key_contributions: Proposed TABSYN, the first latent-diffusion-based framework for mixed-type tabular data synthesis.
Introduced transformer-based tokenizers and decoders to capture inter-column relationships.
Developed an adaptive β-scheduling strategy for stabilizing VAE training and improving latent structure quality.
Proved that a linear noise schedule minimizes reverse approximation errors, enabling fast sampling with fewer than 20 NFEs.
Performed extensive benchmarking on six real-world datasets across multiple fidelity, utility, and privacy metrics, achieving significant performance gains.
evaluation_metrics: Column-wise Density Estimation (Kolmogorov-Smirnov for numeric, TVD for categorical)
Pair-wise Column Correlation (Pearson/Contingency Similarity)
Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST)
Machine Learning Efficiency (AUC, RMSE on XGBoost models)
Privacy (Distance to Closest Record, DCR)
High-order statistics: α-Precision and β-Recall

main_results.fidelity: TABSYN reduced column-wise distribution error by 86% and pair-wise correlation error by 67% compared to best baselines. Achieved 1.08% avg. column density error vs 7.72% for STaSy and 14.52% for TabDDPM.
main_results.utility: Achieved top downstream ML performance (AUC≈0.915, RMSE≈0.58) across datasets, outperforming GAN-, VAE-, and diffusion-based baselines.
main_results.efficiency: Generated data of optimal quality using <20 denoising steps, compared to 50-1000 for prior models, due to linear noise scheduling.
main_results.privacy: High novelty and low DCR confirm that TABSYN samples from learned distributions rather than copying real records.
main_results.visualization: Produced most accurate column density and correlation heatmaps (closest match to real data) among all baselines.

limitations: Requires a two-stage training pipeline (VAE + diffusion), which increases model complexity.
No formal differential privacy guarantees; relies on empirical privacy via DCR.
Adaptive β-scheduling hyperparameters (βmax, βmin, λ) require careful tuning.
Training sensitive to latent dimension (d, D) and tokenizer design.
Not evaluated on multi-table relational data or temporal tabular structures.
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - TABSYN represents a direct advancement in diffusion-based tabular data synthesis, tackling mixed-type modeling, correlation preservation, and sampling efficiency. The latent-space diffusion design and adaptive β-VAE mechanism directly align with my research direction on improving rare-event fidelity, efficiency, and privacy in synthetic tabular data generation. Its architecture (Transformer-VAE + score-based latent diffusion) offers both conceptual and architectural grounding for extending to rare-event-aware or privacy-preserving frameworks.

metadata.title: Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations
metadata.authors: Yang Song, Jascha Sohl-Dickstein, Diederik P. Kingma, Abhishek Kumar, Stefano Ermon, and Ben Poole

papers.abstract: This paper unifies diffusion probabilistic models and score-based generative models under a common stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework. It defines a forward SDE that diffuses data into noise and a reverse-time SDE that reconstructs data from noise using the learned score (gradient of log density). The authors propose flexible sampling techniques, including Predictor-Corrector samplers and a probability flow ODE, enabling both efficient sampling and exact likelihood computation. The approach achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple image generation benchmarks and introduces new capabilities for controllable generation and inverse problem solving.
papers.problem_statement: Existing diffusion and score-based generative models operate under discrete noise levels, limiting sampling flexibility and theoretical understanding. The connection between Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) and Score Matching with Langevin Dynamics (SMLD) was not fully formalized. This paper addresses these gaps by formulating both approaches as continuous-time stochastic processes governed by SDEs, providing a unified mathematical foundation that supports more efficient and flexible generative modeling.
papers.proposed_method: The framework defines a forward SDE that gradually adds noise to data and a reverse-time SDE that removes it, reconstructing data from pure noise. A neural network learns the time-dependent score ∇x log p_t(x), which governs the reverse SDE. The method introduces Predictor-Corrector samplers that combine numerical SDE solvers with score-based MCMC corrections, improving sampling accuracy. Additionally, the authors derive a deterministic Probability Flow ODE equivalent to the reverse SDE, enabling exact likelihood computation and efficient adaptive sampling. The approach also generalizes to conditional sampling tasks such as inpainting and colorization.
key_contributions: Unified diffusion and score-based models within a continuous-time SDE framework.
Derived the reverse-time SDE and Probability Flow ODE enabling both sampling and likelihood estimation.
Introduced Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers for improved sample quality and numerical stability.
Demonstrated controllable generation for tasks like inpainting, colorization, and conditional sampling.
Achieved record-breaking image generation results (FID 2.20, IS 9.89 on CIFAR-10).
datasets_used: LSUN Bedroom, Church, and Cat datasets
evaluation_metrics: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
Inception Score (IS)
Negative Log-Likelihood (bits/dim)
Sampling efficiency (number of function evaluations, NFE)

main_results.fidelity: Achieved FID = 2.20 and IS = 9.89 on CIFAR-10, outperforming DDPM (FID = 3.17, IS = 9.46).
main_results.likelihood: Achieved 2.99 bits/dim on CIFAR-10 with sub-VP SDE, setting a new record for exact likelihood computation.
main_results.efficiency: Adaptive ODE sampling reduced the number of function evaluations by 90% with no perceptual loss in quality.
main_results.controllability: Successfully demonstrated class-conditional generation, inpainting, and colorization using unconditional score models.
main_results.unification: Showed DDPM and SMLD as discretized instances of variance-preserving (VP) and variance-exploding (VE) SDEs, respectively.

limitations: Sampling remains slower than GANs due to iterative denoising steps.
Framework primarily evaluated on image data; applications to other domains (e.g., tabular data) are unexplored.
Requires careful tuning of diffusion coefficients and solver parameters for stability.
Computationally heavy during training due to score network evaluation at multiple time steps.
papers.relevance_justification: HIGHLY RELEVANT - This is a cornerstone paper for diffusion-based generative modeling. It provides the mathematical framework and unification that underlies modern diffusion and score-based models. The SDE and probability flow concepts directly inspire recent tabular adaptations (e.g., TabDDPM, TabDiff) and are critical to understanding the continuous-time formulation used in my proposed research on privacy-fidelity-rare event synthesis. Essential theoretical reference for the diffusion backbone.

metadata.title: SiloFuse_ Cross-silo synthetic data generation with latent tabular diffusion models.
TabDDPM Modelling Tabular Data with Diffusion Models
TABDDPM_ MODELLING TABULAR DATA WITH DIFFUSION MODELS
TABDIFF- A MIXED-TYPE DIFFUSION MODEL FOR TABULAR DATA GENERATION
Adversarial removal of demographic attributes from text data.
Calibrating Noise to Sensitivity in Private Data Analysis
metadata.authors: Cynthia Dwork, Frank McSherry, Kobbi Nissim, Adam Smith

papers.abstract: This paper extends privacy-preserving statistical database research by moving beyond simple 'noisy sums' to handle general query functions. The authors propose that privacy can be preserved by calibrating the random noise added to a query's answer according to the query's 'sensitivity'. Sensitivity is defined as the maximum amount any single database row can change the function's output. This new analysis shows that for many applications, substantially less noise is needed than previously thought. The paper also provides a clean characterization of privacy in terms of indistinguishability.
papers.problem_statement: Previous work on differentially private data analysis focused on 'noisy sums' (where a function is applied to each row and the results are summed). This does not cover many general data analysis functions (e.g., histograms, covariance matrices, minimum cut size). The challenge is to find a general method for adding noise to *any* query function, f, such that privacy is protected, while ensuring the noise is not excessively large and scales appropriately with the function's properties rather than, for example, the dimension of the data.
papers.proposed_method: The paper introduces the concept of **L1 Sensitivity** (S(f)) of a function, f, defined as the maximum L1-norm change in the function's output that can be caused by changing a single row in the database. The proposed privacy mechanism, which ensures **epsilon-indistinguishability** (an early term for differential privacy), is to compute the true answer f(x) and add random noise drawn from the **Laplace distribution** with scale (S(f) / epsilon). The core principle is calibrating the magnitude of the added noise directly to the sensitivity of the function being computed.
key_contributions: Defined **L1 Sensitivity** as a fundamental property of a function, which measures the maximum impact a single individual's data can have on its output.

Proposed the **Laplace Mechanism**: the fundamental DP mechanism that adds noise from the Laplace distribution, with the noise scale calibrated to the function's L1 Sensitivity and the privacy budget (epsilon).
Proved that the Laplace Mechanism provides a strong, provable privacy guarantee, termed 'epsilon-indistinguishability' (which evolved into 'differential privacy').

Showed that for many common, high-dimensional functions (like histograms, covariance matrices, and min-cut), the L1 sensitivity is surprisingly low (e.g., S(f)=2 for a histogram, independent of the number of bins).
Demonstrated that this sensitivity-based analysis allows for adding substantially less noise (e.g., O(1) instead of O(d)) for high-dimensional queries compared to previous methods.
datasets_used: Not applicable. This is a foundational theoretical work. It uses conceptual examples like histograms, covariance matrices, and graph-based queries (e.g., minimum cut size) to demonstrate the method.
evaluation_metrics: **Privacy:** 'epsilon-indistinguishability' (now known as (epsilon, 0)-Differential Privacy), which bounds the log-ratio of probabilities of any transcript (output) on adjacent databases.
**Utility:** The magnitude of the noise added, which is proportional to S(f) / epsilon. Lower sensitivity implies less noise and higher utility.

main_results.privacy_guarantee: The mechanism of adding Laplace noise scaled to S(f) / epsilon is proven to be epsilon-indistinguishable (i.e., (epsilon, 0)-differentially private).
main_results.sensitivity_of_histograms: The L1 sensitivity of a histogram function is 2, regardless of the number of bins (d). This means the noise required scales with O(1), not O(d) or O(sqrt(d)) as in previous work.
main_results.sensitivity_of_covariance_matrices: The L1 sensitivity of a covariance matrix (with bounded elements) is also shown to be low and independent of the dimension, allowing for a significant reduction in added noise.
main_results.general_low_sensitivity: Two broad classes of functions were shown to have low sensitivity: functions that estimate distance to a property (like min-cut) and functions with low sample complexity (that can be approximated from a small random sample).

limitations: The paper focuses on the L1 sensitivity and the Laplace mechanism. (A later appendix in the full 'Algorithmic Foundations' monograph extends this to L2 sensitivity and the Gaussian mechanism).
The analysis of 'functions with low sample complexity' provides a loose bound on sensitivity.

The work shows a separation between interactive (online) and non-interactive (offline) mechanisms, proving that no single non-interactive mechanism can accurately answer all low-sensitivity queries unless the database is exponentially large.
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT. This is a landmark paper that provides the *mechanism* to achieve the formal privacy guarantees my research requires. My research problem is built on 'formal guarantees such as Differential Privacy (DP)', and this paper introduces the **Laplace Mechanism**, which is the fundamental algorithm for achieving DP for numeric queries. It establishes the direct link between the **sensitivity** of a function and the amount of **noise** required, which is the core mathematical tool for DP. This paper explains *how* DP is practically achieved, forming the technical backbone of my 'Privacy' pillar.

metadata.title: Data Synthesis based on Generative Adversarial Networks
metadata.authors: Noseong Park, Mahmoud Mohammadi, Kshitij Gorde, Sushil Jajodia, Hongkyu Park, Youngmin Kim

papers.abstract: Privacy is a major concern when sharing data. Existing techniques like removing identifiers or perturbing values suffer from potential information leakage and poor data utility. This paper proposes table-GAN, a method using generative adversarial networks (GANs) to synthesize fake tables that are statistically similar to the original data, aiming to balance privacy and utility. The method demonstrates 'model compatibility,' where machine learning models trained on the synthetic data achieve performance similar to models trained on the original data.
papers.problem_statement: Traditional data anonymization and perturbation techniques fail to meet both privacy and utility requirements simultaneously. Anonymization methods can still leak private information to attackers with background knowledge, while perturbation methods adversely impact data utility. Furthermore, generative models must be strong against re-identification, attribute disclosure, and membership attacks to be truly useful.
papers.proposed_method: The paper proposes table-GAN, a generative adversarial network specialized for synthesizing tables with mixed data types (categorical, discrete, continuous). The architecture consists of three neural networks: a generator (G) to create synthetic records, a discriminator (D) to distinguish real from fake records, and an auxiliary classifier (C) to increase the semantic integrity of synthetic records (e.g., preventing records like 'cholesterol=60.1, diabetes=1'). The generator is trained using a combination of three loss functions: an original GAN loss, an 'information loss' to match the first and second-order statistics of features, and a 'classification loss' from the auxiliary classifier to maintain semantic correctness.
key_contributions: Proposed table-GAN, a GAN-based method specialized for synthesizing tabular data.
Introduced an architecture with an auxiliary classifier (C) to maintain the semantic integrity and consistency of generated records.
Designed two novel loss functions, 'information loss' and 'classification loss', to improve statistical similarity and semantic correctness.
Demonstrated that table-GAN provides a strong balance between privacy (resisting re-identification, attribute disclosure, and membership attacks) and 'model compatibility' (utility).
Included a hinge loss mechanism (delta_mean, delta_sd) in the information loss to control the trade-off between synthesis quality and privacy level.
datasets_used: LACity (Los Angeles city government employees, 15000 records)
Health (Blood test results, survey, diabetes, 9813 records)
Airline (Bureau of Transportation Statistics ticket data, 1,000,000 records)
evaluation_metrics: Privacy: Distance to the Closest Record (DCR) and a customized Membership Attack success rate (F-1 and AUCROC).
Data Utility (Statistical Comparison): Comparison of cumulative distributions for individual attributes.

Data Utility (Model Compatibility): Machine learning score similarity, specifically F-1 score for classification and Mean Relative Error (MRE) for regression, comparing models trained on real vs. synthetic data.

main_results.statistical_similarity: table-GAN with a low-privacy setting produced more realistic cumulative distributions and a wider range of values than DCGAN and the condensation method.
main_results.model_compatibility_utility: table-GAN showed the best and most reliable model compatibility, with F-1/MRE scores often clustering near the diagonal x=y line, outperforming ARX and sdcMicro in some cases (e.g., Health dataset).
main_results.privacy_dcr: table-GAN (low-privacy) showed significantly larger average DCRs (e.g., 0.96 on LACity, 1.84 on Adult) compared to ARX (0) and sdcMicro (0.07 on LACity) for sensitive attributes, indicating much lower information leakage.
main_results.privacy_membership_attack: The high-privacy setting (delta_mean=0.2, delta_sd=0.2) successfully reduced membership attack success, dropping F-1 score from 0.51 to 0.19 on the Adult dataset.

limitations: The method is specialized for tables with categorical, discrete, and continuous values; other data types (like strings) are left as future work.
The membership attack concept (from related work) was designed for classification algorithms and required customization to attack table-GAN.
Existing risk evaluation metrics (prosecutor, journalist models) could not be applied because table-GAN does not create equivalence classes or one-to-one record correspondence.
The training time, while claimed to be fast (20 mins), relies on a multi-chunk parallel approach for large tables, which is a specific implementation choice for scalability.
papers.relevance_justification: HIGHLY RELEVANT. This paper proposes a foundational GAN-based model (table-GAN) for tabular data, making it a key baseline competitor to my diffusion-based approach. It directly addresses the 'Privacy' (DCR, MIA resistance) and 'Fidelity/Utility' (model compatibility, statistical similarity) pillars of my research. While it doesn't explicitly name 'rare-event fidelity,' its use of an auxiliary classifier and information loss to preserve semantic integrity and statistics is related to maintaining complex patterns. The main deviation is the methodology (GAN vs. Diffusion) and the lack of formal DP guarantees, instead relying on a controllable hinge-loss for privacy.

metadata.title: Deep Learning with Differential Privacy
metadata.authors: Martín Abadi, Andy Chu, Ian Goodfellow, H. Brendan McMahan, Ilya Mironov, Kunal Talwar, Li Zhang
metadata.venue: CCS '16: 23rd ACM Conference on Computer and Communications Security

papers.abstract: Machine learning with neural networks achieves remarkable results but often requires large, sensitive datasets. To prevent exposing private information, this paper develops new algorithmic techniques for learning and a refined analysis of privacy costs within the differential privacy (DP) framework. The authors demonstrate the ability to train deep neural networks with non-convex objectives under a modest privacy budget, with manageable costs in complexity, efficiency, and model quality.
papers.problem_statement: Training deep neural networks requires large, often crowdsourced, datasets that may contain sensitive information. The trained models must not expose this private information. While regularization may incidentally help, models with large capacity can memorize training data, and attacks like model-inversion can extract it. The challenge is to train complex, non-convex deep learning models with rigorous DP guarantees against strong adversaries (who may know the training mechanism and access parameters) without incurring an impractical loss in model quality or training efficiency.
papers.proposed_method: The paper proposes **Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD)**. At each training step, the algorithm computes the gradient for each individual example in a 'lot' (a random subset of data), clips the L2-norm of each per-example gradient to a fixed bound C, and then adds Gaussian noise (scaled to C) to the *average* of the clipped gradients before applying the parameter update. To track the cumulative privacy cost of this iterative process, the paper introduces the **Moments Accountant**, a new analysis technique that tracks the higher moments of the privacy loss random variable. This provides a much tighter (more accurate) bound on the total (epsilon, delta)-DP cost than standard composition theorems.
key_contributions: Introduced the **Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD)** algorithm, a practical method for training deep neural networks with DP.

Developed the **Moments Accountant**, a new privacy accounting method that provides much tighter estimates of the cumulative privacy loss for iterative algorithms (like SGD) compared to standard composition theorems.
Demonstrated the ability to train deep neural networks (with non-convex objectives and millions of parameters) with a modest 'single-digit' privacy budget.
Provided an implementation in TensorFlow and showed empirically that DP-SGD incurs only a manageable cost in software complexity, training efficiency, and model quality on benchmark tasks.
datasets_used: CIFAR-100 (Used as a public dataset to pre-train convolutional layers for the CIFAR-10 experiment)
evaluation_metrics: **Privacy:** (epsilon, delta)-DP, computed via the Moments Accountant vs. Strong Composition.
**Utility:** Model accuracy (test set accuracy) on classification tasks.
**Efficiency:** Training time (epochs).

main_results.moments_accountant_superiority: The Moments Accountant provides a much tighter privacy bound than strong composition. For example, at T=100 epochs, it computes epsilon ≈ 1.26, whereas strong composition gives a much looser bound of epsilon ≈ 9.34.
main_results.mnist_performance: Achieved 97% test accuracy with (8, 10e-5)-DP. Also achieved 90% accuracy with (0.5, 10e-5)-DP.
main_results.cifar10_performance: Achieved 73% test accuracy with (8, 10e-5)-DP (compared to a non-private baseline of 80-86%).
main_results.dp_as_regularizer: DP-SGD acts as a regularizer, resulting in a small difference between training and test accuracy, in contrast to non-private training which overfits.
main_results.hyperparameter_sensitivity: Model accuracy was found to be sensitive to lot size and noise scale, but counterintuitively, *larger* networks (more hidden units) did not decrease accuracy and were more tolerant to noise.

limitations: The per-example gradient computation, while optimized, has overhead. The implementation does not support convolutional layers efficiently, requiring them to be pre-trained on public data.
The gap in accuracy between the private model and the non-private baseline on CIFAR-10 (approx. 7%) is larger than on MNIST (approx. 1.3%), leaving room for future improvements.
The moments accountant requires fixing the number of iterations and privacy parameters ahead of time to avoid risks associated with adaptive parameter choice.
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT. This is the foundational paper for *how* to train deep generative models (like GANs and diffusion models) with Differential Privacy. My research problem requires 'formal guarantees such as Differential Privacy (DP)'. This paper provides the core algorithm (DP-SGD) and the essential privacy accounting method (Moments Accountant) that are used by *all* modern DP deep learning models, including those I will be building and benchmarking. It is the fundamental mechanism for achieving the 'Privacy' pillar of my research.

metadata.title: Differentially Private Data Generation with Missing Data
metadata.authors: Shubhankar Mohapatra, Jianqiao Zong, Florian Kerschbaum, Xi He
metadata.venue: PVLDB (Proceedings of the VLDB Endowment), Vol. 17, No. 8, pp. 2022-2035

papers.abstract: Despite several works that succeed in generating synthetic data with differential privacy guarantees, they are inadequate for generating high-quality synthetic data when input data has missing values. This work formalizes the problems of DP synthetic data with missing values and proposes three effective adaptive strategies that significantly improve utility of synthetic data on four real-world datasets with different types and levels of missing data and privacy requirements. The paper also identifies the relationship between privacy impact for complete ground truth data and incomplete data, and models missing mechanisms as a sampling process to obtain tighter privacy bounds for ground truth data.
papers.problem_statement: Existing DP synthetic data generation mechanisms only work in scenarios where input data has no missing values, but real-world data commonly has missing values (0.2%-78.6% in healthcare datasets). Missing data significantly affects quality of synthetic data generated by DP mechanisms, with 4%-18.5% decay in F1-score with just 10% missing values. The decay varies depending on missing mechanism types (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and data generation processes. The challenge is to generate high-quality DP synthetic data from incomplete datasets while understanding privacy implications for both incomplete and ground truth data.
papers.proposed_method: Three adaptive recourse approaches tailored to different DP mechanism categories: (1) GAN-based adaptive recourse (DP-MisGAN): Trains two generator-discriminator pairs-one for data distribution, one for missingness pattern, using gradient sanitization with WGAN framework and sampled Gaussian mechanism for privacy; (2) Partial marginal observation (PrivBayesE): Enhanced PrivBayes that computes distributions from non-missing values only, discarding rows with missing values on a per-marginal basis rather than globally; (3) Column-wise imputation (KaminoI): Enhanced Kamino that learns attributes sequentially and uses intermediate models to both impute missing values and generate synthetic data in the same process. Additionally, the paper models missing mechanisms as sampling processes and develops privacy amplification techniques using sampling amplification theory to obtain tighter privacy bounds for ground truth data.
key_contributions: First to formalize and study the problems of DP synthetic data generation with missing data, showing 5-23% utility decrease with ≤5% missing values
Developed three novel adaptive approaches (DP-MisGAN, PrivBayesE, KaminoI) tailored to existing DP mechanism categories, improving utility by 15-72%
Differentiated privacy guarantees for incomplete vs ground truth data, developing sufficient conditions for algorithms on incomplete data to achieve privacy for ground truth
First to apply amplification due to missing mechanisms (MCAR) to tighten privacy bounds, showing amplified privacy is 0.1-0.65x the incomplete data privacy with 10-50% missing
Comprehensive evaluation on four real-world datasets with multiple missing types (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and missing percentages (1-30%)
datasets_used: Adult dataset (32,561 rows, combination of numerical and categorical columns)
All datasets artificially injected with 1-30% missing values under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR mechanisms
evaluation_metrics: Average variational distance between k-way marginals (k=1,2) of ground truth and synthetic data
Average F1 scores of 9 classification models trained on synthetic data for each attribute classification task
All experiments repeated 3 times with mean and standard deviation reported
Privacy amplification factors calculated for different missing percentages

main_results.adaptive_vs_baseline: Adaptive methods (DP-MisGAN, PrivBayesE, KaminoI) significantly outperform complete-row baselines: 1-way scores improved up to 68%, 2-way up to 66%, F1-scores up to 24%
main_results.missing_impact: Complete row approach causes 5-23% utility decrease with ≤5% missing, 10-190% decrease with ≤20% missing values
main_results.no_missing_parity: Adaptive methods often achieve same utility as no-missing baseline with 10% missing data (e.g., KaminoI and PrivBayesE on Adult 2-way at 10% MCAR)
main_results.privacy_regime: PrivBayesE performs better at low privacy regime (ε≤1), KaminoI better at higher privacy regime (ε>1)
main_results.privacy_amplification: With MCAR, amplified ground truth privacy decreases almost linearly: 0.88x at 10% missing to 0.44x at 50% missing (Adult), 0.83x to 0.31x (BR2000)
main_results.missing_mechanism: Number of complete rows has more vital impact than missing mechanism type itself; MAR with same rows (MAR-SR) performs similarly to MCAR/MNAR

limitations: No straightforward winning algorithm across all scenarios - performance depends on dataset, downstream task, and privacy budget
Design space for handling missing data is huge; comprehensive solution remains challenging
Privacy amplification analysis limited to MCAR mechanism; MAR and MNAR extensions remain open problems due to conditional dependencies
Identifying missing data types (MAR vs MNAR) without domain knowledge lacks foolproof methods
Algorithm 4 for optimal partition search has exponential complexity; only practical for small number of attributes
High computational cost for some approaches, especially with large missing percentages
papers.relevance_justification: EXTREMELY RELEVANT - This paper uniquely addresses a critical real-world challenge: missing data in privacy-preserving synthetic data generation. Missing data is pervasive in sensitive domains (finance, healthcare) where my research is applicable. The three adaptive approaches provide practical solutions for different types of DP mechanisms that I would consider in my framework. The distinction between privacy for incomplete vs ground truth data and the privacy amplification analysis add theoretical rigor. The comprehensive evaluation across missing types (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and percentages provides actionable insights. This directly supports the privacy and utility pillars of my research while addressing a practical deployment challenge. The only limitation is that it doesn't explicitly address rare-event preservation, but the techniques for handling partial data could potentially help preserve information about rare events that might otherwise be discarded.

metadata.title: Differentially Private Denoise Diffusion Probability Models
metadata.authors: Zhiguang Chu, Jingsha He, Dongdong Peng, Xing Zhang, Nafei Zhu

papers.abstract: Diffusion models and variants have achieved high-quality image generation without adversarial training, providing new solutions for data shortages. However, diffusion models face the same problem as other generative models: the learned probability density function retains characteristics of training samples, and high network complexity makes models easily memorize training samples. When applied to sensitive datasets, this may reveal private information. This paper proposes DPDM (Differentially Private Denoise Diffusion Probability Models) that satisfies differential privacy by adding appropriate noise to gradients during training, along with optimization strategies including adaptive gradient clipping and dynamic learning rate decay.
papers.problem_statement: Despite success in generating high-quality data, diffusion models face privacy leakage risks when trained on sensitive datasets. The learned probability density function will retain characteristics of training samples, and high complexity of deep networks makes models easily remember training samples. This becomes more pronounced when diffusion models are applied to sensitive datasets where the distribution the model focuses on may reveal private information. The challenge is to achieve differential privacy guarantees while maintaining the utility and diversity of generated synthetic data.
papers.proposed_method: DPDM is based on Improved Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (IDDPM) and incorporates differential privacy through the DP-SGD algorithm. The key components include: (1) Gradient clipping with adaptive threshold using g̅ = g/max(1, ||g||₂) instead of traditional clipping with fixed C, leveraging WGAN framework with gradient penalty to naturally bound gradients close to 1; (2) Gaussian noise injection N(0, σ²) added to clipped gradients where σ is chosen based on desired privacy budget ε and δ; (3) Dynamic learning rate decay strategies tested (linear, exponential, logarithmic) to balance convergence speed and model accuracy; (4) Privacy accounting using Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP) for tight privacy loss composition over training iterations.
key_contributions: Proposed DPDM privacy-preserving framework combining differential privacy with diffusion models for synthetic data generation
Introduced adaptive gradient clipping threshold that eliminates need for hyperparameter tuning of clipping bound C
Demonstrated series of optimization strategies including gradient threshold dynamic clipping and learning rate degradation algorithms
Achieved state-of-the-art performance on model utility under same privacy budget compared to baselines
Provided tight privacy analysis using RDP showing (α, ε)-RDP guarantees for the training procedure
datasets_used: MNIST (60,000 training + 10,000 test samples, 28x28 grayscale images, 10 classes)
Fashion-MNIST (60,000 training + 10,000 test samples, 28x28 grayscale images, 10 classes)
CelebA (178x218 RGB face images with 40 binary attributes)
evaluation_metrics: Inception Score (IS): measures quality and diversity by calculating KL divergence between conditional distribution and class distribution
Fréchet Inception Distance (FID): measures distance between feature representations of real and synthetic images (lower is better)
Classification Accuracy: trains classifiers (Logistic Regression, MLP, CNN) on 30k synthetic images and evaluates on real test set

main_results.inception_score: DPDM achieved Inception Scores closer to real data compared to baselines (DP-CGAN, DP-MERF, G-PATE). On MNIST at ε=10, IS≈8.5 (real=9.5), Fashion-MNIST IS≈5 (real=8.5), CelebA IS≈1.7 (real=2)
main_results.fid_score: Lower FID scores indicating better quality: MNIST FID≈125 (baseline ≈180), Fashion-MNIST FID≈130 (baseline ≈250), CelebA FID≈150 (baseline ≈480)
main_results.classification_utility: Classification accuracy on synthetic data: MNIST LR=91%/MLP=95%/CNN=97%, Fashion-MNIST LR=81%/MLP=83%/CNN=88% at ε=10
main_results.learning_rate_impact: Logarithmic decay performed best with FID=127 on MNIST at ε=10, δ=10⁻⁵, outperforming linear (FID=134), exponential (FID=141), and no decay (FID=154)
main_results.adaptive_clipping: Gradient sanitization with WGAN framework eliminated need for manual tuning of clipping threshold C while maintaining privacy guarantees

limitations: Evaluation only on image datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CelebA), not validated on tabular data which has different characteristics
No explicit handling of mixed-type data (categorical and numerical features together)
Privacy budget needs careful allocation - trade-off between privacy (small ε) and utility still exists
Requires significant computational resources for training with 1000 diffusion timesteps
Learning rate decay strategy needs to be selected based on dataset characteristics - no universal best strategy
No evaluation on rare events or class imbalance scenarios
papers.relevance_justification: HIGHLY RELEVANT - This paper directly addresses differential privacy for diffusion models, which is a core component of my research. The adaptive gradient clipping technique and dynamic learning rate strategies are optimization approaches that can be adapted for tabular data generation. The RDP-based privacy accounting provides rigorous theoretical foundations. However, the limitation is that it focuses exclusively on image data rather than tabular data, so the techniques would need adaptation for mixed-type tabular features. The privacy-utility tradeoff analysis and optimization strategies are valuable insights that transfer to my research on privacy-preserving tabular data synthesis.

metadata.title: Differentially Private Regression with Gaussian Processes
metadata.authors: Michael T. Smith, Max Zwiessele, Mauricio A. Álvarez, Neil D. Lawrence

papers.abstract: A major challenge in machine learning is increasing data availability while respecting individual privacy. This paper combines the provable privacy guarantees of differential privacy (DP) with the flexibility of Gaussian processes (GPs). The authors propose a method for DP regression using GPs and improve it by crafting the DP noise covariance structure ("cloaking") to efficiently protect training data while minimizing added noise. This cloaking method achieves greater accuracy while maintaining privacy guarantees.
papers.problem_statement: Applying machine learning to personal data requires balancing data utility with individual privacy. Simple anonymization is insufficient. The goal is to apply the rigorous guarantees of differential privacy to Gaussian process regression, specifically to protect the training *outputs* (e.g., income, health information) while assuming the *inputs* (e.g., locations, ages) are public. A naive application of DP noise is often too large, making the results unusable.
papers.proposed_method: The paper first applies DP to a GP by bounding the change (sensitivity) in the GP's posterior mean function when one training output is perturbed. It then adds scaled Gaussian DP noise based on this bound. To improve this, the paper introduces the **Cloaking Method**. This method assumes the test (query) locations are known in advance. It then calculates the optimal noise covariance structure that specifically 'cloaks' the effect of changing any single training point on the test predictions, minimizing the total added noise while still providing (epsilon, delta)-DP.
key_contributions: Proposed a novel method to provide (epsilon, delta)-differentially private regression using Gaussian processes, protecting training outputs.
Developed an improved 'cloaking' method that requires *a priori* knowledge of test inputs to craft an optimal DP noise covariance structure.
Demonstrated that the cloaking method minimizes the scale of added noise, achieving greater accuracy and utility for a given privacy guarantee compared to a standard DP GP or binning methods.
Provided a practical DP method for regression over multi-dimensional inputs, allowing the release of the GP's covariance structure (as it's non-private) so users can account for uncertainty.
datasets_used: !Kung San women height and age data (287 samples)
New York City Citi Bike journey data (June 2016, 1.46M journeys)
evaluation_metrics: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) between DP predictions and true values.
Visual comparison of posterior mean and DP noise samples.
Comparison of DP noise scale (transparency in Figure 3).

main_results.standard_method_inefficiency: The standard DP-GP method (without cloaking) adds excessive noise, overwhelming the function estimate even with a very high epsilon (e.g., epsilon=100).
main_results.cloaking_method_superiority: The cloaking method provides practical DP perturbation and achieves the lowest RMSE compared to simple binning and integral binning methods, especially at stricter privacy levels (lower epsilon).
main_results.noise_distribution: The cloaking method intuitively adds less DP noise in areas with high data concentration (where neighbors 'protect' each other) and more noise in sparse areas. Noise also approaches zero far from the data, as predictions revert to the prior mean regardless of training data.
main_results.hyperparameter_cost: Selecting hyperparameters in a DP way (using the exponential mechanism) adds a small, manageable cost, slightly increasing the RMSE.

limitations: The primary cloaking method requires knowing the test (query) input points *a priori*.
The method primarily protects training outputs (y), not inputs (X). Protecting inputs is mentioned as needing a general bound on the inverse-covariance function.
The DP hyperparameter optimization method described (using the exponential mechanism) is simple but 'far from optimal'.
The method for releasing the corrupted mean function (e.g., as a server or as samples) is not fully resolved.
Extending the method to GP classification is noted as an 'obvious next step' but not implemented.
papers.relevance_justification: MODERATELY RELEVANT. This paper is highly relevant to the 'Privacy' and 'Fidelity' aspects of the research problem, as it provides a rigorous method for DP regression (a utility task) with provable guarantees. It directly addresses the privacy-utility trade-off. However, it is **not** a *generative* method for *synthetic data*; it is a method for private *prediction*. It also does not use diffusion models and does not address tabular data's mixed-type nature or the specific challenge of rare events (though it does note DP noise is higher in 'rarefied' data regions).

metadata.title: DIFFERENTIALLY PRIVATE SYNTHETIC DATA VIA FOUNDATION MODEL APIS 1_ IMAGES
Differentially Private Tabular Data Synthesis using Large Language Models
metadata.authors: Toan V. Tran, Li Xiong

papers.abstract: Synthetic tabular data generation with differential privacy (DP) is crucial for sharing sensitive data. This paper introduces DP-LLMTGen, a novel framework that uses pretrained large language models (LLMs) for this task. DP-LLMTGen employs a two-stage fine-tuning process with a new loss function designed for tabular data. Empirical evaluations show it outperforms existing mechanisms across multiple datasets and privacy settings.
papers.problem_statement: Developing DP tabular data generators that provide realistic synthetic data remains challenging. Naively applying DP-SGD to fine-tune existing LLM-based methods fails because the injected noise breaks the data's format compliance. Furthermore, the standard cross-entropy loss used in language modeling is not well-suited for the structure and numerical properties of tabular data.
papers.proposed_method: The paper proposes DP-LLMTGen, a framework that fine-tunes a pretrained LLM in two stages. **Stage 1 (Format Learning)** fine-tunes the LLM on randomly generated (non-sensitive) tabular data using standard causal language modeling to learn the data format (feature names, categories, ranges) without spending any privacy budget. **Stage 2 (Differentially Private Fine Tuning)** fine-tunes the model on the real sensitive data using DP-SGD and a novel loss function. This loss function combines a Weighted Cross Entropy Loss (WCEL), which focuses more on data tokens than format tokens, and a Numerical-Understanding Loss (NUL), which uses squared error to better capture the magnitude of numerical errors.
